Project Description

Explaining Metlock as a modular zinc recovery project.

Metlock is a synthetic biology project built around a simple idea: if zinc recovery could be made more controllable, more selective, and easier to interpret, biology might become a useful partner in metal-rich environments and future biomining workflows.

This page is the front-facing overview of the project. It explains the problem, why zinc matters, how the modules connect, and where the platform could go next.

Explore the Modules View Human Practices
Project Overview

What Metlock is trying to do.

Metlock is a modular synthetic biology platform for zinc recovery and metal-rich environments. Rather than treating zinc handling as one isolated experiment, the project is organized as a small system: one module focuses on import, one on calibration, and one on zinc binding.

That modular structure helps the team ask clearer questions. How strongly should zinc uptake be turned on? How can expression be compared across conditions? How can binding be measured in ways that stay interpretable as the project grows? Metlock is built to make those questions easier to answer.

Problem Context

Why this problem matters.

Metal recovery is important for industry, technology, and environmental cleanup, but conventional methods can be energy intensive, chemically harsh, and difficult to adapt to smaller or more variable streams. That creates a gap where biological approaches may eventually help by adding selectivity, controllability, or flexibility.

Metlock focuses on that gap. The project does not assume biology will replace existing recovery systems outright. Instead, it explores whether engineered cells and shared measurement tools can contribute useful zinc-handling behavior in a way that is more tunable and easier to compare over time.

Project framing Metlock is designed as a platform for reasoning about zinc recovery, not only as a single final construct.
Why Zinc Recovery Matters

Why zinc is the first focus.

Zinc is a useful starting point because it is both industrially relevant and biologically meaningful. It matters in metal-rich waste streams, environmental contexts, and future discussions of biomining, but it also creates real questions about toxicity, transport, and cellular burden.

That makes zinc a good test case for a modular system. If Metlock can help define how to tune uptake, compare expression behavior, and study binding strategies around zinc, the same design logic could later support broader work on related metals.

Metlock’s Modular Approach

How the project is organized.

Module 1 focuses on tunable zinc import using AHL-responsive control of ZnuABC. Module 2 uses sfGFP calibration and promoter comparison to understand expression before moving into more demanding functional proteins. Module 3 explores zinc-binding systems that could support extracellular or intracellular capture strategies.

Each module has its own role, but the real value comes from how they connect. The project becomes stronger when import is calibrated, calibration is reusable, and binding strategies can be interpreted against the same broader framework.

Figure placeholder Add a platform overview diagram or module relationship figure here.
How the Three Modules Work Together

A shared project logic rather than separate subprojects.

The modules are meant to support one another. Calibration helps define safe and interpretable expression windows. Import work provides a functional zinc-handling test. Binding work broadens the possible recovery strategies and gives the team more than one way to think about metal capture.

This structure makes Metlock easier to explain to new visitors and easier to extend in future iterations. Instead of one long technical story with no internal structure, the project becomes a coordinated platform with clear roles for each part.

Future Direction

Where the project could go next.

The long-term value of Metlock lies in reuse. Future work could add more complete experimental datasets, refine calibration logic, compare new binding candidates, or adapt the framework to other metals and other environmental conditions.

Even in its current form, the project is already structured to support that growth. The design emphasizes modularity, clearer interpretation, and documentation that future teams can build on rather than replace.

METLOCK

A Missouri-Miners iGEM 2025 project focused on programmable zinc capture, computational design support, and environmentally responsible recovery workflows.

Wiki Scope

Built as a structured scientific record for design, testing, modeling, outreach, and documentation across the 2025 competition season.
